message:
fix(charmed_log): eliminate double-lock race window in log() (bd-3lk7)

Replace multiple lock acquisitions with single write lock for atomic
format+write operation in log(). This eliminates the race window where
configuration could change between formatting and writing.

Changes:
- Consolidate warning check, format, and write into single write lock scope
- Add associated function versions of format methods (format_text_inner,
  format_json_inner, format_logfmt_inner) to avoid &self borrow conflicts
- Keep original instance methods as wrappers for backward compatibility

Also closes epic bd-3ju1 (charmed_log Thread Safety & Reliability Hardening)
as all child beads are now complete.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-28jp","title":"demo_showcase UX: keybindings + interaction model","description":"Define an opinionated interaction model that feels premium:

- Global keys (examples):
  - `?` help overlay, `q` quit, `esc` back/close, `/` search/command palette, `t` theme switcher, `tab` focus next pane.
- Per-widget conventions: list/table navigation, viewport scrolling, form submit/cancel.
- Mouse expectations: click to focus/select; wheel scroll in viewports/lists.

Acceptance:
- A single canonical keymap that pages/components conform to (avoid per-page random keys).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:07:05.332580358Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T01:08:24.185343532Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T01:08:24.185278631Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","input","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-28jp","depends_on_id":"bd-33mv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:17:01.082895581Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-28jp","depends_on_id":"bd-3kcq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:07:05.356173194Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-28l3","title":"Wizard: implement huh form UI embedded in app","description":"Implement the Wizard page using huh components, embedded inside the demo_showcase app chrome.

Why:
- This is the "do the thing" workflow: it makes the demo feel like a real deployment/onboarding tool.
- It showcases huh’s strengths: multi-step forms, validation, confirm/select flows.

Requirements:
- Multi-step flow as designed in `Wizard: design the multi-step workflow`:
  - a mix of input types (text, select, confirm)
  - validations with clear inline errors
- Navigation:
  - cancel/back behavior matches global UX rules (`esc` closes / backs out safely)
  - submit produces a clear success/failure state
- Presentation:
  - keyboard-first
  - mouse optional
  - visually cohesive with the app theme

Integration:
- On completion, the wizard triggers the shared domain action API (separate task) to create a deployment/job and emit logs/toasts.

Acceptance:
- Wizard feels like a real onboarding/deploy tool, not a demo form.
- Validation and recovery flows are polished (errors feel intentional).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:11:01.916099726Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:31:59.740408640Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","forms","huh"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-28l3","depends_on_id":"bd-1ynf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:41.872484594Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-28l3","depends_on_id":"bd-2k35","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:44.500650602Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-28l3","depends_on_id":"bd-2omz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:11:01.934899534Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-28l3","depends_on_id":"bd-7jxu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:47.373969167Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

The Rust Style has these methods; we just need to apply them based on the test name.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T16:43:48.947027377Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T16:46:51.173555122Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T16:46:51.173490171Z","close_reason":"Implemented partial border edges support in lipgloss conformance tests. border_partial_top_bottom now passes. Lipgloss conformance is now 58/58 (was 57/58 with 1 skip).","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-28wz","depends_on_id":"bd-212m","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T16:43:48.960863628Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-299e","title":"E2E: structured test logging + artifact capture","description":"Add an E2E logging + artifacts foundation so failures are instantly diagnosable.

Why:
- TUIs are notoriously hard to debug from CI logs alone.
- We want every failure to answer: "what inputs happened", "what screen did we see", "what did we assert".

Deliverables:
- A `TestLogger`/`ScenarioRecorder` that records *structured* events:
  - scenario name + run id
  - step index + human-readable step description
  - injected input (key/mouse/resize) including raw bytes when relevant
  - assertions performed + expected vs actual summaries
  - captured output snapshots (last N frames + final frame)
  - active Config snapshot (seed/theme/toggles, artifacts dir)
- Output formats:
  - `events.jsonl` (machine readable) with stable keys for tooling
  - `summary.txt` (human readable) that points to the exact failing step
- Artifact directory structure (deterministic and CI-friendly):
  - `target/demo_showcase_e2e/<scenario>/<run_id>/...`
  - always write artifacts on failure; optionally keep them on success behind an env flag

Logging schema guidance (keep it simple and grep/jq-friendly):
- Required fields: `ts`, `level`, `scenario`, `run_id`, `step`, `event`, `message`
- Optional fields: `input`, `assertion`, `expected`, `actual`, `frame_path`, `config`

Acceptance:
- A failing E2E test leaves a clear trail: "what happened" + "what we expected" + the last visible screen.
- The artifact bundle is enough to reproduce locally (seed + scenario name + config snapshot).","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:30.556281120Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:09:28.910592210Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","logging","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-299e","depends_on_id":"bd-pfqw","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:30.586010319Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2b7h","title":"Unit tests: simulation determinism (no real sleeps)","description":"Add unit tests proving the demo_showcase background simulation is deterministic and test-friendly.

Why:
- The demo depends on a "fake backend" simulation. If it’s nondeterministic, tests become flaky.
- E2E scenarios also rely on deterministic evolution (tick-driven, no real sleeps).

Coverage:
- Given a fixed seed and a fixed tick delta:
  - advancing N ticks produces expected state changes (jobs progress, metrics trends, log entries)
- No wall-clock sleeps:
  - the simulation can be stepped manually (pure tick function)
- Stability:
  - no NaNs/overflow/panics with extreme tick counts or dt values

Acceptance:
- The simulation is deterministic and easy to reason about.
- Tests run fast and do not require a TTY.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:34:06.578799968Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T01:27:15.528802419Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T01:27:15.528736406Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2b7h","depends_on_id":"bd-2zeq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:34:06.591863269Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2b7h","depends_on_id":"bd-y3c8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:34:20.337957210Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2eky","title":"demo_showcase core: guided tour / walkthrough mode","description":"High-impact demo feature: a guided walkthrough that showcases capabilities without the user needing prior knowledge.

Behavior:
- A key (e.g. `g`) starts a step-by-step tour.
- Each step highlights a UI region and prompts an action (or auto-navigates after a delay).
- Explains what the current screen demonstrates (bubbletea, lipgloss, bubbles, glamour, huh, etc.).

Acceptance:
- A first-time user can run the tour and leave convinced this stack is real and powerful.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:22:54.239302138Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:04.532386317Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","polish","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2eky","depends_on_id":"bd-2k35","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:02.079116118Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2eky","depends_on_id":"bd-3j9x","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:22:54.254258582Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2eky","depends_on_id":"bd-7jxu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:04.532354107Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2eky","depends_on_id":"bd-zc72","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:03.311920339Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2f52","title":"Unit tests: shell-out action safety (headless no-op, Cmd shape)","description":"Add unit tests for the demo_showcase shell-out capability.

Why:
- Shell-out is powerful but easy to make flaky (hanging pager, broken terminal restore).
- We need to guarantee CI/headless runs never block.

Test coverage:
- In headless/custom-IO mode (e.g., `--self-check`), triggering the shell-out action:
  - does *not* spawn a pager/process
  - returns quickly
  - emits a clear structured log/toast explaining it was skipped
- Cmd structure:
  - the action composes its work with `bubbletea::sequence(...)` so release/run/restore ordering is explicit
  - release + restore messages are present in the expected order when not headless

Acceptance:
- Tests run without a TTY and without external processes.
- Failures show exactly which invariant broke (ordering, headless guard, etc.).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:08:30.981035716Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:08:31.002031497Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bubbletea","demo_showcase","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2f52","depends_on_id":"bd-194c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:08:31.002015177Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2f52","depends_on_id":"bd-2zeq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:08:30.996944015Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3j9x","title":"demo_showcase: core TUI shell + router (bubbletea)","description":"Implement the reusable application shell that everything else plugs into.

Key ideas:
- One top-level App model that owns routing + global concerns (theme, status, notifications).
- Pages are sub-models/components; App delegates update/view and composes commands.
- Strong separation between domain data (fake backend) and presentation.

Acceptance:
- App boots into a multi-pane layout with navigation and at least one page.
- Clean message routing (no spaghetti downcasts everywhere).","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:04.314919933Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:04.333853721Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","bubbletea","demo_showcase"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3j9x","depends_on_id":"bd-yqh4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:04.333820659Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3jg2","title":"Interactions: bracketed paste support in inputs/search","description":"Bubbletea delivers paste sequences as `KeyMsg` with `paste=true` (and/or via terminal paste events).

Why:
- Paste is a "real app" interaction: users paste queries, IDs, paths, and commit hashes.
- Terminals can deliver large paste payloads; naïve handling can lag or break inputs.

Ensure paste works sensibly in:
- Jobs query bar
- Logs query/filter input
- Docs search
- Wizard text fields (huh)
- Notes scratchpad (textarea)

Edge cases:
- Very large paste:
  - should not freeze the UI
  - inputs should apply paste in a bounded way (e.g., respect char limits)
- Multi-line paste:
  - for search inputs: collapse whitespace/newlines into spaces (or clearly defined behavior)
  - for textarea: preserve newlines

Acceptance:
- Pasting a multi-word query "just works" across inputs.
- No hangs or panics with large paste payloads.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:12:37.767936662Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:53.947449304Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","input","paste"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3jg2","depends_on_id":"bd-114p","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:12:37.786648712Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3jg2","depends_on_id":"bd-2tl1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:17:58.203351507Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-3ju1","title":"charmed_log: Thread Safety & Reliability Hardening","description":"# Epic: charmed_log Thread Safety & Reliability Hardening

## Background
The charmed_log crate provides structured logging with lipgloss styling. During deep review,
several critical issues were identified that can cause application crashes or silent data loss.

## Critical Issues Identified

### 1. Lock Poisoning on RwLock::unwrap() - CRITICAL
**Location**: Lines 427, 468, 475, 481, 488, 494, 500, 506, 512, 518, 528, 579, 597
**Problem**: Every read/write lock acquisition uses .unwrap() which panics if lock is poisoned.
Lock poisoning occurs when a thread panics while holding a lock.
**Impact**: A single panic in any formatting routine breaks ALL future logging in the application.
**Risk**: Production reliability - one panic → application crash cascade

### 2. Silent I/O Error Discarding - CRITICAL
**Location**: Line 598: `let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());`
**Problem**: Write failures to stderr go completely unnoticed (disk full, permission denied, pipe closed).
**Impact**: Logs silently disappear without any notification.
**Risk**: Silent data loss in production - critical audit logs may vanish

### 3. Backtrace Overhead in Caller Reporting - HIGH
**Location**: Line 308: `let bt = Backtrace::new();`
**Problem**: Every log call with report_caller=true captures and parses full stack trace.
**Impact**: 100x+ slower than regular logging when caller reporting is enabled.
**Risk**: Severe performance degradation if accidentally enabled in production

### 4. Double Lock Pattern Race Window - MEDIUM
**Location**: Lines 579-598
**Problem**: log() acquires read lock, formats, drops, then acquires write lock.
Configuration could change between the two lock acquisitions.
**Impact**: Possible inconsistency in output formatting under concurrent configuration changes.

## Design Philosophy for Fixes
- Logging should be resilient - never crash the application
- Errors should be reported through a configurable error handler
- Performance-critical paths should have explicit opt-in for expensive features
- Thread safety should use recover-from-poisoning strategy for logging

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All lock acquisitions handle poisoning gracefully
- [ ] I/O errors are propagated to configurable error handler
- [ ] Backtrace overhead is documented with performance warning
- [ ] All changes have regression tests
- [ ] Zero clippy warnings","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:10.326116387Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:10.342807374Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["charmed_log","critical","thread-safety"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ju1","depends_on_id":"bd-gyzr","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:10.342713910Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-3ju1","title":"charmed_log: Thread Safety & Reliability Hardening","description":"# Epic: charmed_log Thread Safety & Reliability Hardening

## Background
The charmed_log crate provides structured logging with lipgloss styling. During deep review,
several critical issues were identified that can cause application crashes or silent data loss.

## Critical Issues Identified

### 1. Lock Poisoning on RwLock::unwrap() - CRITICAL
**Location**: Lines 427, 468, 475, 481, 488, 494, 500, 506, 512, 518, 528, 579, 597
**Problem**: Every read/write lock acquisition uses .unwrap() which panics if lock is poisoned.
Lock poisoning occurs when a thread panics while holding a lock.
**Impact**: A single panic in any formatting routine breaks ALL future logging in the application.
**Risk**: Production reliability - one panic → application crash cascade

### 2. Silent I/O Error Discarding - CRITICAL
**Location**: Line 598: `let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());`
**Problem**: Write failures to stderr go completely unnoticed (disk full, permission denied, pipe closed).
**Impact**: Logs silently disappear without any notification.
**Risk**: Silent data loss in production - critical audit logs may vanish

### 3. Backtrace Overhead in Caller Reporting - HIGH
**Location**: Line 308: `let bt = Backtrace::new();`
**Problem**: Every log call with report_caller=true captures and parses full stack trace.
**Impact**: 100x+ slower than regular logging when caller reporting is enabled.
**Risk**: Severe performance degradation if accidentally enabled in production

### 4. Double Lock Pattern Race Window - MEDIUM
**Location**: Lines 579-598
**Problem**: log() acquires read lock, formats, drops, then acquires write lock.
Configuration could change between the two lock acquisitions.
**Impact**: Possible inconsistency in output formatting under concurrent configuration changes.

## Design Philosophy for Fixes
- Logging should be resilient - never crash the application
- Errors should be reported through a configurable error handler
- Performance-critical paths should have explicit opt-in for expensive features
- Thread safety should use recover-from-poisoning strategy for logging

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All lock acquisitions handle poisoning gracefully
- [ ] I/O errors are propagated to configurable error handler
- [ ] Backtrace overhead is documented with performance warning
- [ ] All changes have regression tests
- [ ] Zero clippy warnings","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:10.326116387Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:00:22.270057599Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:00:22.269990013Z","close_reason":"All child beads complete: bd-3lwk (error handler), bd-1bja (lock poisoning), bd-2go9 (caller overhead docs), bd-3lk7 (double-lock fix). charmed_log now has robust thread safety and reliability.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["charmed_log","critical","thread-safety"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ju1","depends_on_id":"bd-gyzr","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:10.342713910Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3kcq","title":"demo_showcase: product concept + UX + visual spec","description":"Define the *product story* and UX blueprint for the demo_showcase app so the implementation is cohesive (not a grab-bag of components).

Deliverables (in-beads, not a separate doc):
- App concept (what it is pretending to be), core user journeys, navigation IA.
- Keybindings + mouse interactions philosophy (keyboard-first, mouse-supported).
- Visual system: spacing scale, typography/weight usage, borders, accent colors, status semantics.
- Accessibility/readability constraints: contrast, focus states, "NO_COLOR"/ASCII fallbacks.

Acceptance:
- All subsequent implementation issues can reference this epic for the intended "feel" and UX rules.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:02.355567902Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:02.371452730Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","design","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3kcq","depends_on_id":"bd-yqh4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:02.371406974Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3kk5","title":"Docs: implement markdown viewer (glamour + viewport)","description":"Implement the Docs page viewer UI: beautiful Markdown rendering in a real app chrome.

Why:
- The Docs page is a flagship glamour showcase and doubles as onboarding.
- It must look great *and* behave like a real reader (fast, searchable, resizable).

Requirements:
- Render markdown with glamour using:
  - current theme
  - current content width
  - syntax/line-number toggles (if enabled)
- Display rendered output inside `bubbles::viewport`.
- Scrolling:
  - smooth line/page scrolling
  - clear scroll position indication (optional)
- Resize:
  - re-render on width changes (avoid re-render loops)

Performance considerations:
- Cache the last rendered output per doc + width + theme/toggles to avoid unnecessary work.

Acceptance:
- Markdown looks great and is readable; scrolling is smooth.
- Resizing produces correct reflow without broken borders.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:41.644067143Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:30:03.289589115Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","docs","glamour","viewport"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3kk5","depends_on_id":"bd-1y5q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:20.700839493Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3kk5","depends_on_id":"bd-22vc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:41.663813227Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3kk5","depends_on_id":"bd-2k35","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:15.479047421Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3kk5","depends_on_id":"bd-39bb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:23.368496686Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3kk5","depends_on_id":"bd-7jxu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:19:18.119084229Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3kvw","title":"Quality: performance sanity pass","description":"Do a performance sanity pass on the showcase to ensure it stays smooth and doesn’t hide O(n^2) traps.

Why:
- A flagship demo must feel snappy; if it’s sluggish, the whole library looks bad.
- This also protects us from accidental performance regressions as features pile on.

What to check:
- Rendering hot paths:
  - avoid rebuilding huge strings when only a small viewport slice changed
  - avoid per-frame allocations in common paths
- Logs page:
  - thousands of lines should remain scrollable
  - filtering/search should not reformat everything per keypress
- Docs page:
  - avoid re-rendering markdown on unrelated keypresses
- Animations:
  - tick scheduling should be bounded and respect reduce-motion/unfocused state

Deliverables:
- A short write-up (in the issue) of findings + any fixes.

-{"id":"bd-3lk7","title":"charmed_log: Fix double-lock race window in log() method","description":"# Task: Fix Double-Lock Race Window in log() Method

## Problem Statement
The log() method has a brief window where configuration could change between
acquiring the read lock (to format) and acquiring the write lock (to write):

\`\`\`rust
// Current implementation
pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    let inner = self.inner.read().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    // ... format output using inner's settings ...
    drop(inner);  // Release read lock
    
    // GAP: Another thread could modify configuration here
    
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
}
\`\`\`

## Race Condition Scenario
1. Thread A calls log(), acquires read lock
2. Thread A reads formatter=JSON, starts formatting
3. Thread A drops read lock
4. Thread B calls set_formatter(Text), acquires write lock
5. Thread B changes formatter to Text, releases lock
6. Thread A acquires write lock
7. Thread A writes JSON-formatted output (but formatter is now Text)

## Impact Analysis
- **Low severity**: Configuration changes during logging are rare
- **Inconsistent output**: Single log entry could use mixed settings
- **Not a crash**: Just potentially confusing output

## Solution Design

### Option A: Hold Read Lock, Capture Writer Handle
\`\`\`rust
pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    // Single write lock for entire operation
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    
    let output = self.format_output(&inner, level, msg, keyvals);
    
    if let Err(e) = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes()) {
        self.handle_write_error(&mut inner, e);
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Option B: Copy-on-Read for Immutable Config
\`\`\`rust
struct LogConfig {
    formatter: Formatter,
    level: Level,
    // ... other immutable config
}

pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    let config = {
        let inner = self.inner.read().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
        inner.config.clone()  // Clone config snapshot
    };
    
    let output = self.format_with_config(&config, level, msg, keyvals);
    
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
}
\`\`\`

### Recommended: Option A
Simpler implementation, single lock acquisition, atomic operation.
Performance cost is minimal (write lock slightly slower than read).

## Implementation Steps
1. Refactor log() to use single write lock
2. Move formatting inside the lock scope
3. Handle error within the same lock scope
4. Ensure no deadlock risk (CallerInfo::capture happens outside lock)
5. Add concurrent test to verify atomicity
6. Benchmark to verify no performance regression

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests
\`\`\`rust
#[test]
fn log_configuration_is_atomic() {
    use std::sync::{Arc, Barrier};
    use std::thread;
    
    let logger = Logger::new();
    logger.set_formatter(Formatter::Json);
    
    let barrier = Arc::new(Barrier::new(3));
    let output = Arc::new(Mutex::new(Vec::new()));
    
    // Thread 1: Change formatter repeatedly
    let logger1 = logger.clone();
    let b1 = barrier.clone();
    let t1 = thread::spawn(move || {
        b1.wait();
        for _ in 0..100 {
            logger1.set_formatter(Formatter::Text);
            logger1.set_formatter(Formatter::Json);
        }
    });
    
    // Thread 2: Log messages
    let logger2 = logger.clone();
    let b2 = barrier.clone();
    let output2 = output.clone();
    let t2 = thread::spawn(move || {
        b2.wait();
        for i in 0..100 {
            logger2.info(&format!("msg{i}"), &[]);
        }
    });
    
    barrier.wait();
    t1.join().unwrap();
    t2.join().unwrap();
    
    // Each log line should be valid JSON or valid Text, never mixed
    let output = output.lock().unwrap();
    for line in String::from_utf8_lossy(&output).lines() {
        assert!(
            serde_json::from_str::<serde_json::Value>(line).is_ok() ||
            !line.starts_with('{'),
            "Malformed line: {line}"
        );
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Performance Benchmark
\`\`\`rust
#[bench]
fn bench_log_single_threaded(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let logger = Logger::new();
    b.iter(|| {
        logger.info("benchmark message", &[("key", "value")]);
    });
}

#[bench]
fn bench_log_multi_threaded(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let logger = Logger::new();
    let logger = Arc::new(logger);
    
    b.iter(|| {
        let handles: Vec<_> = (0..4).map(|_| {
            let l = logger.clone();
            thread::spawn(move || {
                for _ in 0..100 {
                    l.info("benchmark", &[]);
                }
            })
        }).collect();
        for h in handles {
            h.join().unwrap();
        }
    });
}
\`\`\`

### E2E Test Script
\`\`\`bash
#!/bin/bash
# Test: charmed_log_race_condition.sh

set -e
echo "=== charmed_log Race Condition Tests ==="

# Run with thread sanitizer if available
if command -v rustup &> /dev/null; then
    RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=thread" cargo test --package charmed_log log_configuration_is_atomic -- --nocapture 2>&1 | tee /tmp/charmed_log_race.log
else
    cargo test --package charmed_log log_configuration_is_atomic -- --nocapture 2>&1 | tee /tmp/charmed_log_race.log
fi

echo "Test output saved to /tmp/charmed_log_race.log"
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] log() uses single lock acquisition
- [ ] Configuration reads and writes are atomic
- [ ] No performance regression (benchmarked)
- [ ] Thread sanitizer passes (no data races)
- [ ] Unit test demonstrates atomic behavior

 {"id":"bd-3lwk","title":"charmed_log: Add configurable error handler for I/O failures","description":"# Bug: Silent I/O Error Discarding

## Problem Statement
Line 598 silently discards all I/O errors:
```rust
let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
```

When writing to stderr fails (disk full, permission denied, pipe closed, etc.),
the error is completely ignored. Critical log messages vanish without any indication.

## Impact Analysis
- **Audit Logs**: Compliance-critical logs may silently disappear
- **Debugging**: Error messages lost when most needed (during failures)
- **Monitoring**: Log-based alerting becomes unreliable
- **Forensics**: Missing logs create gaps in incident investigation

## Scenarios Where This Fails
1. Disk full on log directory
2. Log file permissions changed
3. Pipe to log collector closed unexpectedly
4. stdout/stderr redirected to closed file descriptor
5. Docker container stdout buffer overflow

## Solution Design

### Option A: Configurable Error Handler (RECOMMENDED)
Add an optional error handler callback:

```rust
pub struct Logger {
    inner: Arc<RwLock<LoggerInner>>,
}

struct LoggerInner {
    // ... existing fields ...
    error_handler: Option<Box<dyn Fn(std::io::Error) + Send + Sync>>,
}

impl Logger {
    pub fn with_error_handler<F>(self, handler: F) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(std::io::Error) + Send + Sync + 'static
    {
        let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
        inner.error_handler = Some(Box::new(handler));
        drop(inner);
        self
    }
}

// In log():
if let Err(e) = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes()) {
    if let Some(ref handler) = inner.error_handler {
        handler(e);
    }
    // Optionally: attempt fallback to stderr if primary fails
}
```

### Option B: Return Result
Not recommended - changes the entire API contract and makes logging
awkward (callers don't want to handle errors from logging).

### Default Behavior
If no error handler is configured:
- First failure: Print warning to stderr (if available)
- Subsequent failures: Silent (to avoid infinite loops)

## Implementation Steps
1. Add error_handler field to LoggerInner
2. Add with_error_handler() builder method
3. Modify log() to call handler on I/O failure
4. Add fallback-to-stderr option
5. Add tests for error handling scenarios
6. Document the error handling behavior

## Testing Strategy
```rust
#[test]
fn error_handler_called_on_io_failure() {
    let error_captured = Arc::new(Mutex::new(None));
    let error_clone = error_captured.clone();
    
    let logger = Logger::new()
        .with_writer(FailingWriter)
        .with_error_handler(move |e| {
            *error_clone.lock().unwrap() = Some(e.to_string());
        });
    
    logger.info("test", &[]);
    
    assert!(error_captured.lock().unwrap().is_some());
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Error handler callback mechanism implemented
- [ ] Default behavior documented (silent after first warning)
- [ ] Tests for various I/O failure scenarios
- [ ] Example in docs showing error handler usage
- [ ] No breaking changes to existing API","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:57.520589539Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:23:21.251959454Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:22:55.633760095Z","close_reason":"Implemented configurable error handler for I/O failures. Added ErrorHandler type, with_error_handler() builder method, proper error handling in log() with default warn-once behavior, and 5 new tests.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["charmed_log","critical","reliability","silent-failure"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3lwk","depends_on_id":"bd-1bja","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:23:21.251928716Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3lwk","depends_on_id":"bd-3ju1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:57.542307390Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3m25","title":"Jobs: details pane (logs, params, timeline)","description":"Implement a job details pane that updates based on the selected job.

- Job summary:
  - status, start time, duration, progress
- Parameters (key/value table):
  - target env, service, commit/version, actor
- Timeline:
  - queued → running → finished
  - show step durations (optional)
- Logs tail:
  - show a small live tail for the selected job
  - clearly indicate when log view is truncated

Implementation notes:
- Use `bubbles::viewport` to keep the pane scrollable.
- Ensure details updates are smooth:
  - switching selection should not flicker
  - preserve scroll position per job (optional but very polished)

Acceptance:
- Selecting different jobs updates details instantly and without flicker.
- Details remain readable in narrow terminals.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:22.109773635Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:48:31.490082452Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:48:31.490015698Z","close_reason":"Jobs details pane with Summary, Parameters, Timeline, and Logs sections","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","jobs","viewport"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3m25","depends_on_id":"bd-1dr6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:22.127890180Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3m25","depends_on_id":"bd-33fe","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:18:54.272699801Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3m25","depends_on_id":"bd-3rwl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:18:51.606950999Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
